name: MacTutor
biography:
The viscount Robert d'Adh횄짤mar , born on the first of November 1874 in Saint Hippolyte-du-Fort ( Gard, France ) , was descended from one of the oldest noble and protestant families from the region of Provence ( South of France ) . His father, Marius d'Adh횄짤mar, worked for the French administration as a collector of taxes ( receveur de l'enregistrement et des domaines ) and, a few years before his son's birth, fought in the Franco-Prussian War of 1870 - 1871 . Robert d'Adh횄짤mar's mother was Louise Willelmine Grand d'Esnon. Although Robert was born into a protestant family and brought up as a protestant, he later converted to become a Roman Catholic.
Robert d'Adh횄짤mar was awarded his baccalaureate in 1890 in Montpellier ( H횄짤rault, France ) . He then studied at the Lyc횄짤e Amp횄짢re at Lyon, which he entered on 1 October 1891 , preparing for the entrance examination for the 횄혟cole Centrale des Arts et Manufactures de Paris. He began his studies at the 횄혟cole Centrale des Arts et Manufactures in 1893 , graduating with an engineering degree in 1896 . He went on to study mathematics on his own and was, therefore, self-taught in mathematics. In April 1898 he wrote to his friend Robert de Montessus de Ballore telling him about the lectures of 횄혟mile Picard that he was attending [ 2 ] :- A word in haste to tell you that I will write at length soon to tell you about the course of M Picard that I am attending regularly - tomorrow is the 10 th lesson. We might make a judgement under these conditions, which was hard after 3 or 4 lessons. The course is wonderful, like his books only easier. In short, one learns properly only with really good books. This is to reassure and encourage you. From 1898 he began teaching at the Saint Fran횄짠ois boarding school in Evreux, about 90 km to the west of Paris, in fact the letter we just quoted from was sent from Evreux. He obtained his Licence es Sciences in 1899 from the Sorbonne. In 1902 , the lectures of 횄혟mile Picard edited by Robert d'Adh횄짤mar were published under the title Le횄짠ons sur les s횄짤ries 횄혻 termes positifs, profess횄짤es au Coll횄짢ge de France 창혫혟 ( Lectures on the series with positive terms, given at the College de France ) . He was elected to the Soci횄짤t횄짤 Math횄짤matique de France on the 21 February 1900 . His candidature was supported by 횄혟mile Picard and Maurice d'Ocagne .
Robert d'Adh횄짤mar defended his thesis on 22 April 1904 before a committee consisting of his supervisor 횄혟mile Picard , Jacques Hadamard and 횄혟douard Goursat . The first part of the thesis, entitled Sur une classe d'횄짤quations aux d횄짤riv횄짤es partielles du second ordre, du type hyperbolique, 횄 3 ou 4 variables ind횄짤pendantes 창혫혟 ( On a class of hyperbolic partial differential equations of the second order with 3 or 4 independent variables ) , dealt with the partial differential equation (1) with limit condition that arise in the two problems: the interior problem and the exterior problem.
In fact, he improved on a work of Vito Volterra on that subject. In the case of the interior problem, Robert d'Adh횄짤mar used his new methods to confirm more quickly the formulas which Volterra had obtained. For this problem, he dealt also with the equation (2) .
The thesis was published in 1905 in the Journal de Math횄짤matiques Pures et Appliqu횄짤es (5) 10 , pp 131 - 207 . In a lecture to the International Congress of Mathematicians at Heidelberg in August 1904 , Hadamard referred to the ideas in Robert d'Adh횄짤mar's thesis.
In 1901 , Robert d'Adh횄짤mar became Ma횄짰tre de Conf횄짤rences ( essentially equivalent to an assistant lecturer ) at the Lille Catholic University. He was promoted in November 1904 to substitute professor, then to full professor in 1907 . He taught differential and integral calculus. In 1902 he helped his friend, the mathematician Robert de Montessus de Ballore , to join the Catholic University of Lille when he proposed him to fill a vacancy as substitute for the Special Mathematics course. Robert de Montessus was, at this time, teaching in Senlis and d'Adh횄짤mar wrote to his friend [ 2 ] :- I do not write to you to influence you but I think you must have the courage to leave Senlis, as I had the audacity to leave Evreux. ... Moreover, in the same letter, Robert d'Adh횄짤mar explained to his friend that the course of Jules Drach at the University of Lille would be of a great interest for him to prepare for the agr횄짤gation .
Robert d'Adh횄짤mar married Jeanne Marie Henriette Duhamel ( born the 15 April 1881 and died the 20 August 1965) on 5 February 1904 in Merville. Jeanne Marie Henriette Duhamel was the daughter of a manufacturer who owned a castle in Merville in the north of France. Robert d'Adh횄짤mar and his wife lived in Lambersart in the suburbs of Lille.
Robert d'Adh횄짤mar became an officer in the general staff of the French Army in Italy during World War I. He wrote to Robert de Montessus from the Italian front on 25 May 1918 [ 2 ] :- My dear friend, I spent one month in Alsace, and I was called urgently to Italy. My training in Alsace was too short ... It was also lovely. I visited all the batteries in an area, and heard the shells whistle, accompanied by a charming man, Gal Jaquet. Here we organize a large ... firing between France and Italy. I deal with the artillery and I have a lot of work under a blazing sun, surrounded by roses. The front has nothing in common with the French front. He left the Lille Catholic University, being given leave during the academic years 1920 - 1921 and 1921 - 1922 , then was appointed as professor at the engineering school Institut Industriel du Nord ( now a day 횄혟cole Centrale de Lille ) in 1922 .
It was in 1901 that Robert d'Adh횄짤mar's first publications were published, namely the mathematical papers Sur une int횄짤gration par approximations successives 창혫혟 ( On integration by successive approximations ) and Sur une classe d'횄짤quations aux d횄짤riv횄짤s partielles du second ordre 창혫혟 ( On a class of partial differential equations of the second order ) . In the same year his 40 -page paper on the history of mathematics L'oeuvre math횄짤matique du XIXe si횄짢cle 창혫혟 ( The mathematical work of the nineteenth century ) also appeared. His first book, however, was on the philosophy of science and religion, topics which occupied him in the early part of his career. The Preface to the book La philosophie des sciences et le probl횄짢me religieux 창혫혟 ( Philosophy of science and the religious problem ) (1904) begins as follows:- The philosophy of science has completely changed its point of view in France in the last ten years of the 19 th century. I would like to show the major importance in this movement of ideas and to perceive what may be their distant consequences. He divides science into three parts, mathematics, physics/chemistry, and biological sciences but, in this book, considers mainly the philosophy of mathematics and physics. The final chapter is on science and religion. He continues this discussion of the ideas in this book in his second book Le triple conflit : science, philosophie, religion (1905) .
His next book Les variations des th횄짤ories de la science 창혫혟 ( Changes in theories of science ) (1907) continued to investigate similar subjects. He writes in the Introduction:- I do not wish to speak ill of my contemporaries. And yet we must admit that when it comes to religious things, most of them lose their composure. But, addressing them, we say that the Catholic Church is the greatest in the history of religion and the Catholic faith lives in a very large number of intellectually elite men of high morals. ... we will be booed three times, held to be of unsound mind and narrow heart. He goes on to say that religious thought varies both with the times and with the individual thinkers.
Although he had been writing philosophy book, he continued to publish mathematical papers such as Sur une 횄짤quation aux d횄짤riv횄짤es partielles du type hyperbolique 창혫혟 ( On a partial differential equation of hyperbolic type ) (1905) and historical works such as Trois ma횄짰tres: Amp횄짢re, Cauchy, Hermite 창혫혟 ( Three masters: Ampere, Cauchy, Hermite ) (1905) . His first mathematics book Les 횄짤quations aux d횄짤riv횄짤es partielles 횄혻 caract횄짤ristiques r횄짤elles 창혫혟 ( Partial differential equations with real characteristics ) was published in 1907 . This was quickly followed by the books Exercices et le횄짠ons d'analyse. Quadratures. 횄혟quations diff횄짤rentielles. 횄혟quations int횄짤grales de M Fredholm et de M Volterra. 횄혟quations aux d횄짤riv횄짤es partielles du second ordre 창혫혟 ( Exercises and lessons of analysis. Quadratures. differential equations. Fredholm and Volterra integral equations. Partial differential equations of the second order ) (1908) and L'횄짤quation de Fredholm et les probl횄짢mes de Dirichlet et de Neumann 창혫혟 ( The Fredholm equation and problems of Dirichlet and Neumann ) (1909) .
In collaboration with Robert de Montessus , he wrote the two volume book Calcul num횄짤rique 창혫혟 ( Numerical calculation ) (1911) . The first volume covered arithmetic and algebraic operations and the second volume studied integration. This book gave numerical methods for solving non-linear equations. Perhaps his most famous book was the two volume Lecons sur les principes de l'analyse par R d'Adh횄짤mar, professeur a la Facult횄짤 Libre des Sciences de Lille 창혫혟 ( Lessons on the principles of analysis of R Adhemar, professor at the Free Faculty of Sciences at Lille ) . The first volume S횄짤ries.- D횄짤terminants.- Int횄짤grales.- Potentiels. Equations int횄짤grales. Equations diff횄짤rentielles et fonctionnnelles 창혫혟 ( Series.- Determinants.- Integrals.- potentials. Integral Equations. functional and differential equations ) was published in 1912 with the second volume Fonctions synectiques, m횄짤thodes des majorantes. Equations aux deriv횄짤es partielles du premier ordre. Fonctions enti횄짢res 창혫혟 ( Synectic functions, the method of majorant. Partial differential equations of the first order. Entire functions ) being published in the following year.
His later books were: Henri Poincar횄짤 (1914) ; R횄짤sistance des mat횄짤riaux 창혫혟 ( Resistance of materials ) (1921) ; and Statique cin횄짤matique 창혫혟 ( Static kinematics ) (1923) which was intended as an introduction to mechanics for engineers. He wrote several works on ballistics such as La balistique ext횄짤rieure 창혫혟 ( Exterior ballistics ) , Gauthier-Villars ( Paris, 1934) and Th횄짤orie du mouvement gyroscopique des projectiles 창혫혟 ( Theory of the gyroscopic movement  of projectiles ) (1939) .
